does: This paper introduces IntentGrasp, a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating intent understanding in Large Language Models. The benchmark is constructed by curating and unifying 49 open-licensed datasets across 12 diverse domains, applying intent label contextualization, and standardizing task formats. IntentGrasp provides a large-scale training set of 262,759 instances alongside two evaluation sets: an All Set with 12,909 test cases and a more challenging Gem Set with 470 carefully selected cases. The authors conduct extensive evaluation of 20 LLMs from 7 different families, including frontier models like GPT-5.4, Gemini-3.1-Pro, and Claude-Opus-4.7, revealing that most models achieve scores below 60% on the All Set and below 25% on the Gem Set, with 17 out of 20 models underperforming a random baseline of 15.2% on the harder Gem Set despite estimated human performance of 81.1%.
connects: This work builds on the long-standing research tradition of intent detection and classification in natural language understanding, which has been central to task-oriented dialogue systems and semantic parsing for decades. It responds to limitations in existing intent understanding benchmarks by addressing the need for larger-scale, multi-domain evaluation resources that reflect diverse communication contexts. The paper extends earlier efforts in benchmark construction for language understanding tasks, drawing on methodologies for dataset curation and unification seen in works studying general language capabilities. It connects to recent work examining the robustness and generalization of LLMs across varied natural language understanding tasks, and builds on the growing literature demonstrating that frontier language models still struggle with structured semantic understanding despite their impressive performance on other tasks.
enables: This benchmark enables future research into improving LLMs' intent understanding capabilities by providing a standardized, large-scale evaluation framework across multiple domains and difficulty levels. The significant performance gap between current models and human-level understanding points toward the need for new training methods, architectural improvements, or fine-tuning approaches specifically targeting intent comprehension. The work supports development of more nuanced evaluation methodologies that go beyond surface-level task completion, potentially influencing how researchers design task-oriented dialogue systems and conversational agents. By establishing the Gem Set as a challenging subset, the benchmark enables researchers to focus on genuinely difficult cases, driving innovation in semantic understanding and context-aware reasoning. The results motivate investigation into why even frontier models fail at intent understanding despite strong general capabilities.
